items.myth: O(n) is always faster than O(n²).
items.reality: Only at large enough n. For small inputs (n ≤ 50 or so) constants and cache locality can make an O(n²) implementation beat an O(n) one. Big-O is an *asymptotic* claim.
items.example:
```python
# For tiny lists, a Python-level linear scan can beat building a set:
if x in [1,2,3,4]:   # 4 comparisons, no hashing overhead
    ...
```

items.reality: Base doesn't matter in Big-O — log₂(n), log₁₀(n) and ln(n) differ only by a constant factor, which Big-O ignores. By convention, in algorithms it's base 2.
items.example:
```
log₂(1024) = 10
log₁₀(1024) ≈ 3.01
# Different numbers, same O(log n) growth class.
```

items.reality: It measures *growth rate* — how time changes when n changes. It says nothing about wall-clock seconds, which depend on hardware, language, cache, and the hidden constants.
items.example:
```
O(n) sum in Python ≈ 50 ns/element
O(n) sum in NumPy ≈ 1 ns/element  (vectorized)
Same complexity, 50× difference.
```

items.reality: Big-O is normally about time. Space complexity is a separate Big-O analysis. A program can be O(n) time and O(n²) space — both numbers matter for production.
items.example:
```python
# Building an n×n similarity matrix:
M = [[sim(a,b) for b in items] for a in items]   # O(n²) time AND O(n²) space
```

items.myth: If two algorithms are both O(n log n), their performance is the same.
items.reality: They're in the same growth class but constants can differ wildly. Timsort beats heapsort beats mergesort beats quicksort on certain inputs, all O(n log n).
items.example:
```python
sorted(xs)      # CPython's Timsort — excellent constants and adaptive
# vs a hand-rolled mergesort in pure Python — same O() but much slower in practice.
```
